sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems
topicArea: Endocrine System II: Hormones, Feedback & Disease

questions.passage: A 45-year-old patient presents to her physician with fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, and dry skin over the past six months. Physical examination reveals bradycardia, a delayed relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes, and a slightly enlarged thyroid gland (goiter). Laboratory results show elevated TSH and low free T4. The physician diagnoses primary hypothyroidism and prescribes levothyroxine (synthetic T4) replacement therapy.
questions.stem: Elevated TSH combined with low free T4 indicates primary hypothyroidism because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: excess T4 is inhibiting TSH production, creating a compensatory increase in free T4

choices.label: B
choices.text: the hypothalamus is overproducing TRH, which directly suppresses thyroid function

choices.label: C
choices.text: the pituitary is failing to produce TSH, causing low thyroid output

choices.label: D
choices.text: the thyroid gland is failing to produce adequate T4, removing negative feedback on the pituitary, which responds by secreting more TSH in an attempt to stimulate thyroid output

explanations.A: Excess T4 would suppress, not stimulate, TSH production through negative feedback. High T4 + high TSH would be inconsistent with normal HPT axis function and could indicate a TSH-secreting pituitary tumor (TSHoma) — a rare condition. The question describes the opposite pattern (low T4, high TSH).
explanations.B: TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) from the hypothalamus stimulates TSH release from the pituitary, which then stimulates thyroid hormone production. High TRH would drive high TSH and likely high T4, not the pattern of high TSH + low T4 seen here. Additionally, TRH does not directly act on the thyroid.
explanations.C: Pituitary failure to produce TSH would cause low TSH and low T4 (secondary hypothyroidism), not high TSH and low T4. If the pituitary fails, it cannot drive thyroid production — both TSH and T4 would be low. The key in this case is that TSH is elevated, indicating the pituitary is working hard to stimulate a failing thyroid.
explanations.D: Correct. In primary hypothyroidism, the thyroid gland itself is defective — it produces insufficient T4 and T3. In a normal HPT axis, T3/T4 provide negative feedback on both the hypothalamus (suppressing TRH) and the pituitary (suppressing TSH). When T4 is low, this negative feedback is removed. The pituitary, sensing inadequate T4, releases more TSH in a compensatory attempt to stimulate the failing thyroid. The hallmark of primary hypothyroidism is therefore high TSH with low T4 — the pituitary is responding normally to a failing gland. This is distinguished from secondary (pituitary failure: low TSH + low T4) and tertiary (hypothalamic failure: low TRH → low TSH → low T4) hypothyroidism.

questions.topic: HPT Axis / Primary Hypothyroidism / Negative Feedback

questions.stem: T4 (thyroxine) is a prohormone that must be converted to T3 (triiodothyronine) to exert its primary biological effects. This conversion occurs primarily by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: peripheral deiodination by deiodinase enzymes — removal of one iodine atom from the outer ring of T4 produces active T3, while removal from the inner ring produces inactive reverse T3 (rT3)

choices.label: B
choices.text: phosphorylation by thyroid hormone receptor kinases in the nucleus

choices.label: C
choices.text: glucuronidation in the liver, which activates T4 by making it more water-soluble

choices.label: D
choices.text: direct conversion by thyroid peroxidase at the follicular cell membrane

explanations.A: Correct. Three deiodinase enzymes (D1, D2, D3) regulate thyroid hormone activation and inactivation. Type 1 (D1) and Type 2 (D2) deiodinases remove iodine from the 5'-outer ring of T4, producing active T3 (~80% of circulating T3 comes from peripheral T4 conversion). D2 is particularly important in target tissues (brain, pituitary, brown fat). Type 3 (D3) removes iodine from the 5-inner ring, producing inactive reverse T3 (rT3). Stress, illness, and fasting increase rT3 production (euthyroid sick syndrome). T3 is ~3-4× more potent than T4 and binds thyroid hormone receptors with much higher affinity.
explanations.B: Thyroid hormone receptors are nuclear receptors (TRα, TRβ) that bind T3 directly. T3 binding to TR changes corepressor to coactivator recruitment, altering transcription. The receptor does not phosphorylate T4 — the hormone is deiodinated (loses an iodine) to become T3. Thyroid hormones are not activated by phosphorylation.
explanations.C: Glucuronidation is a Phase II detoxification reaction that makes molecules more water-soluble for renal excretion — it inactivates (not activates) thyroid hormones. Glucuronide conjugates of thyroid hormones are excreted in bile and urine. This is a major pathway for T4/T3 metabolism and elimination, not activation.
explanations.D: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) is an enzyme in thyroid follicular cells that organifies iodide (I⁻ → active iodine) and couples iodotyrosines on thyroglobulin to form T3 and T4 within the thyroid gland. It does not convert T4 to T3 at the follicular membrane — that conversion occurs in peripheral tissues by deiodinases.

questions.topic: Thyroid Hormone / T4 to T3 Conversion / Deiodinases

questions.stem: Type 1 diabetes mellitus results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells. The absence of insulin causes:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decreased glucagon secretion, reducing blood glucose by suppressing hepatic glucose output

choices.label: B
choices.text: hyperglycemia, increased lipolysis (producing ketone bodies that can cause diabetic ketoacidosis), and negative nitrogen balance due to protein catabolism — because insulin normally promotes glucose uptake, inhibits lipolysis, and promotes protein synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: exclusively increased glycogen synthesis in the liver, normalizing blood glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: decreased glucose absorption from the GI tract, which is the primary mechanism of hyperglycemia

explanations.A: Insulin normally suppresses glucagon secretion from α-cells. Without insulin, glucagon secretion increases (not decreases), driving even more hepatic glucose output (glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis), worsening hyperglycemia. Unregulated glucagon in the absence of insulin is a major contributor to diabetic ketoacidosis.
explanations.B: Correct. Insulin is a pleiotropic anabolic hormone with multiple effects: (1) promotes GLUT4-mediated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue; (2) promotes glycogen synthesis and inhibits glycogenolysis in liver and muscle; (3) inhibits lipolysis in adipose tissue (by inhibiting hormone-sensitive lipase); (4) promotes protein synthesis and inhibits protein catabolism. Without insulin: glucose uptake fails → hyperglycemia; lipolysis increases → fatty acids released → liver converts to ketone bodies (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate) → ketoacidosis; protein catabolism increases → negative nitrogen balance and muscle wasting. Glucagon, cortisol, and growth hormone (counter-regulatory hormones) are unopposed, further worsening hyperglycemia.
explanations.C: Without insulin, glycogen synthesis is impaired (glycogen synthase is less active) and glycogenolysis is increased (glucagon-stimulated). The liver produces more glucose, not less. Glycogen synthesis cannot normalize blood glucose in the absence of insulin.
explanations.D: GI glucose absorption (via SGLT1 in the intestinal epithelium and GLUT2 in portal blood) does not require insulin. Glucose is absorbed normally from the gut in type 1 diabetes — in fact, glucose absorption continues normally but peripheral uptake fails because insulin-responsive GLUT4 transporters are not translocated to the cell surface without insulin signaling.

questions.stem: Cortisol is a glucocorticoid produced by the adrenal cortex in response to stress. Its metabolic effects include:

choices.label: A
choices.text: promoting glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue by stimulating GLUT4 translocation

choices.label: B
choices.text: decreasing hepatic gluconeogenesis and increasing insulin sensitivity

choices.label: C
choices.text: inhibiting protein synthesis throughout the body

choices.label: D
choices.text: raising blood glucose by stimulating hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis, inhibiting peripheral glucose uptake, and mobilizing substrates through proteolysis and lipolysis

explanations.A: GLUT4 translocation is stimulated by insulin, not cortisol. In fact, cortisol opposes insulin action — it decreases insulin sensitivity and inhibits glucose uptake in peripheral tissues. Chronic corticosteroid therapy causes hyperglycemia (steroid-induced diabetes) by opposing insulin's actions.
explanations.B: Cortisol increases hepatic gluconeogenesis (by inducing PEPCK, glucose-6-phosphatase) and decreases insulin sensitivity — the opposite of what this answer states. Cortisol is a counter-regulatory hormone that elevates blood glucose during stress.
explanations.C: Cortisol does increase protein catabolism (especially in muscle) to provide amino acids as gluconeogenic substrates, but this is not the same as globally inhibiting protein synthesis. Cortisol increases synthesis of some proteins (hepatic enzymes, gluconeogenic enzymes, acute-phase proteins) while reducing protein synthesis in muscle and lymphoid tissue.
explanations.D: Correct. Cortisol's primary metabolic role is to ensure adequate blood glucose during stress (the 'fight or flight' resource mobilization): (1) stimulates hepatic gluconeogenesis by inducing enzymes (PEPCK, pyruvate carboxylase) and providing substrates; (2) stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis; (3) inhibits glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue (insulin resistance); (4) promotes lipolysis in adipose tissue, providing fatty acids as alternative fuel and glycerol as gluconeogenic substrate; (5) promotes proteolysis in muscle, providing amino acids for gluconeogenesis. Net effect: blood glucose rises (hyperglycemia). Chronic excess cortisol (Cushing's syndrome) causes central obesity, muscle wasting, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, and immunosuppression.

questions.stem: Growth hormone (GH) secretion from the anterior pituitary is regulated by opposing hypothalamic hormones. GH secretion increases in response to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: sleep (especially deep NREM sleep), fasting, exercise, and stress — conditions associated with increased metabolic demand or low nutrient availability

choices.label: B
choices.text: hyperglycemia after a meal, which signals adequate nutrient availability

choices.label: C
choices.text: IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor), which stimulates more GH in a positive feedback loop

choices.label: D
choices.text: somatostatin (SRIF) from the hypothalamus, which stimulates pituitary GH release

explanations.A: Correct. GH secretion is pulsatile and highest during deep NREM sleep (especially slow-wave sleep stages N3), which is why adequate sleep is important for growth and tissue repair. GH also increases with fasting (low glucose and low amino acids signal the need for metabolic mobilization), exercise (acute stress), physical or psychological stress, and puberty. These stimuli trigger GHRH (and ghrelin) release from the hypothalamus, while the negative feedback loop involves IGF-1 (produced by the liver in response to GH) suppressing both GH and GHRH.
explanations.B: Hyperglycemia after a meal suppresses GH secretion. GH is a counter-regulatory hormone — it antagonizes insulin action (raises blood glucose). After eating (when glucose is already high), GH is suppressed to avoid further hyperglycemia. Fasting (low glucose) stimulates GH secretion, promoting lipolysis and protein-sparing effects.
explanations.C: IGF-1 (produced by the liver under GH stimulation) exerts negative feedback — it suppresses GH secretion from the pituitary and GHRH from the hypothalamus. This is a classic negative feedback loop, not positive. In acromegaly (excess GH from a pituitary tumor), elevated IGF-1 should suppress GH but fails to do so because the tumor is autonomous.
explanations.D: Somatostatin (somatotropin release-inhibiting factor, SRIF) inhibits GH secretion from the pituitary — it is the opposing hormone to GHRH (growth hormone-releasing hormone, which stimulates GH). The name 'SRIF' indicates its inhibitory function. Exogenous somatostatin analogues (octreotide) are used to treat acromegaly by suppressing excess GH.

questions.topic: Growth Hormone Regulation / GH Secretion Stimuli

questions.stem: Aldosterone is a mineralocorticoid that regulates blood pressure by acting on the distal nephron. It is released primarily in response to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: high blood glucose detected by adrenal cortex cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: ACTH from the pituitary, which is the sole regulator of aldosterone production

choices.label: C
choices.text: low blood osmolarity, which signals the need for more sodium reabsorption

choices.label: D
choices.text: high extracellular K⁺ concentration and angiotensin II — both directly stimulate the adrenal zona glomerulosa to produce aldosterone, increasing Na⁺ reabsorption and K⁺ excretion

explanations.A: The adrenal cortex does not have glucose sensors analogous to pancreatic β-cells. Glucose is not a direct regulator of aldosterone secretion. Aldosterone is regulated primarily by the RAAS (angiotensin II) and potassium concentration, not by blood glucose.
explanations.B: ACTH does stimulate aldosterone (as well as cortisol) in the adrenal cortex, but it is not the sole or primary regulator. The major regulators of aldosterone are angiotensin II (from the RAAS cascade: renin → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II) and hyperkalemia. ACTH is more important for cortisol regulation; RAAS and K⁺ are more important for aldosterone.
explanations.C: Low blood osmolarity (hypo-osmolarity) stimulates ADH (vasopressin) release, not aldosterone. ADH increases water reabsorption; aldosterone increases Na⁺ reabsorption. The triggers are different: osmolarity and volume for ADH; angiotensin II, K⁺, and volume for aldosterone.
explanations.D: Correct. Aldosterone is regulated by two primary stimuli: (1) Angiotensin II — produced when renin (from juxtaglomerular cells in response to low renal perfusion, low NaCl delivery to the macula densa, or sympathetic activation) cleaves angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II. Angiotensin II acts on zona glomerulosa cells to increase aldosterone synthesis; (2) Hyperkalemia — directly depolarizes zona glomerulosa cells, stimulating aldosterone release. Aldosterone increases Na⁺ reabsorption (via ENaC) and K⁺ secretion (via ROMK) in the distal nephron/collecting duct. This expands blood volume (Na⁺ retains water) and lowers serum K⁺.

questions.stem: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) is secreted when plasma calcium falls. PTH raises blood calcium by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: inhibiting osteoclast activity, which prevents bone resorption and retains calcium in bone

choices.label: B
choices.text: stimulating calcium loss in the urine to restore extracellular calcium balance

choices.label: C
choices.text: acting on bone (stimulating osteoclast-mediated resorption), kidneys (increasing Ca²⁺ reabsorption and activating vitamin D), and indirectly on the GI tract (via activated vitamin D increasing Ca²⁺ absorption)

choices.label: D
choices.text: directly binding intestinal enterocytes to increase calcium absorption without requiring vitamin D

explanations.A: PTH activates (not inhibits) osteoclast-mediated bone resorption. PTH binds receptors on osteoblasts, which then signal osteoclasts (via RANKL/OPG pathway) to resorb bone and release Ca²⁺ and phosphate into the bloodstream. Inhibiting osteoclasts would cause calcium to remain in bone and blood calcium would fall further.
explanations.B: PTH increases calcium reabsorption in the kidney (at the distal tubule), not calcium loss. PTH promotes calcium retention in the body by stimulating: (1) renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption (reducing urinary Ca²⁺ loss), (2) renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase activation (converting 25-OH-D → 1,25-(OH)₂-D, active vitamin D), and (3) bone resorption. Urinary calcium loss is reduced, not increased, by PTH.
explanations.C: Correct. PTH acts through a coordinated three-organ system: (1) Bone — PTH binds osteoblast PTH1R receptors → osteoblasts produce RANKL → RANKL binds RANK on osteoclast precursors → osteoclast activation → bone resorption → Ca²⁺ and PO₄ released; (2) Kidney — PTH increases Ca²⁺ reabsorption in the distal tubule, decreases phosphate reabsorption (phosphaturia), and activates 1α-hydroxylase in the proximal tubule to produce calcitriol (1,25-(OH)₂D₃); (3) Gut (indirectly) — calcitriol produced in response to PTH acts on intestinal enterocytes to upregulate TRPV5/6 and calbindin-D9k, increasing Ca²⁺ absorption. Net effect: blood Ca²⁺ rises, blood phosphate falls.
explanations.D: PTH does not directly act on intestinal enterocytes. PTH receptors are not expressed on intestinal cells. The intestinal effect of PTH is entirely indirect — PTH stimulates the kidney to produce calcitriol (active vitamin D), which is the hormone that directly upregulates intestinal calcium transporters.

questions.stem: The posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis) secretes ADH and oxytocin. These hormones differ from most other pituitary hormones in that they are:

choices.label: A
choices.text: synthesized in the anterior pituitary and secreted through the posterior lobe

choices.label: B
choices.text: lipid-soluble steroid hormones that diffuse freely across cell membranes

choices.label: C
choices.text: synthesized in hypothalamic nuclei (supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei), transported along axons to the posterior pituitary, and released into the circulation from axon terminals

choices.label: D
choices.text: regulated by portal blood from the anterior pituitary to the posterior pituitary

explanations.A: ADH and oxytocin are not synthesized in the anterior pituitary. The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) produces TSH, ACTH, GH, FSH, LH, and prolactin — all different hormones with a different embryological origin and cell type. The posterior pituitary is composed of axon terminals from hypothalamic neurons, not secretory cells.
explanations.B: ADH (vasopressin) and oxytocin are small peptide hormones (9 amino acids each), not lipid-soluble steroids. They bind G-protein-coupled receptors on cell surfaces (V1R, V2R for ADH; OTR for oxytocin) and act through second messenger cascades. Steroid hormones are derived from cholesterol and diffuse into cells to bind intracellular receptors.
explanations.C: Correct. ADH and oxytocin are synthesized in the cell bodies of magnocellular neurons in the hypothalamic supraoptic nucleus (ADH predominant) and paraventricular nucleus (oxytocin and ADH). These neurons project their axons through the pituitary stalk to the posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis). The hormones are transported down the axons packaged in secretory granules, stored in axon terminals in the posterior pituitary, and released into fenestrated capillaries in response to action potentials. The posterior pituitary is therefore a neural structure (part of the brain), not a true endocrine gland.
explanations.D: The hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system delivers hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones from the median eminence to the anterior pituitary capillaries, regulating anterior pituitary hormone secretion. The posterior pituitary is a neural extension of the hypothalamus — it communicates via axonal action potentials, not via portal blood flow.

questions.stem: Epinephrine (adrenaline) is released from the adrenal medulla during the stress response. Its effects include:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decreasing heart rate, dilating blood vessels in skeletal muscle, and lowering blood glucose to conserve energy for recovery

choices.label: B
choices.text: increasing heart rate (β₁), dilating airways (β₂), constricting skin and gut vessels (α₁), dilating skeletal muscle vessels (β₂), increasing glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis — mobilizing resources for fight or flight

choices.label: C
choices.text: exclusively acting on the heart by binding cardiac muscarinic receptors to increase force of contraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: stimulating insulin secretion to promote glucose uptake into cells during the stress response

explanations.A: Epinephrine increases, not decreases, heart rate (positive chronotropy via β₁ receptors). During a stress response, the cardiovascular system is activated to deliver more oxygen to working muscles. Vasodilation in skeletal muscle occurs via β₂ receptors, but vasoconstriction occurs in skin, gut, and kidney (α₁). Blood glucose is increased by epinephrine (glycogenolysis), not lowered.
explanations.B: Correct. Epinephrine activates adrenergic receptors throughout the body, coordinating the integrated 'fight or flight' response: (β₁) Heart — increased rate and contractility (positive chronotropy and inotropy); (β₂) Lung — bronchodilation (increases airway caliber for more O₂ delivery); (β₂) Skeletal muscle vessels — vasodilation (increases blood flow to working muscles); (α₁) Skin, gut, kidney vessels — vasoconstriction (redirects blood from non-essential organs to muscles and heart); (β₁/β₂ on liver) — glycogenolysis; (α₁ on adipose) — lipolysis; (β₂ on pancreatic β-cells) — inhibits insulin secretion. This coordinated response maximizes energy availability and physical performance during acute stress.
explanations.C: Epinephrine binds adrenergic receptors (α and β subtypes), not muscarinic receptors. Muscarinic receptors bind acetylcholine (parasympathetic neurotransmitter). Cardiac muscarinic receptor (M2) activation actually decreases heart rate — the opposite of epinephrine's β₁ effect.
explanations.D: Epinephrine inhibits insulin secretion via α₂ adrenergic receptors on pancreatic β-cells (not stimulates it). During stress, the body needs to maintain high blood glucose for brain and muscle function, so insulin release is suppressed. Simultaneously, glucagon secretion from α-cells is promoted by epinephrine (β₂), further raising blood glucose.

questions.stem: Negative feedback is the primary regulatory mechanism of most endocrine axes. In the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, high levels of estrogen during the late follicular phase of the menstrual cycle shift from negative to positive feedback, triggering:

choices.label: A
choices.text: suppression of FSH and LH, ending follicular development

choices.label: B
choices.text: the LH surge, which triggers ovulation approximately 36–40 hours later

choices.label: C
choices.text: progesterone release from the corpus luteum before ovulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: decreased estrogen synthesis as a protective mechanism against overstimulation

explanations.A: Estrogen typically does suppress FSH and LH (negative feedback) throughout most of the cycle. However, when estrogen rises above a critical threshold for a sustained period (late follicular phase), the feedback switches from negative to positive. The LH surge (and a smaller FSH surge) follows — it does not suppress FSH/LH at this critical moment.
explanations.B: Correct. The LH surge is the defining hormonal event of the menstrual cycle that triggers ovulation. When estrogen (primarily estradiol from the dominant follicle) exceeds ~200 pg/mL for >36 hours, it switches the hypothalamus and pituitary from negative to positive feedback — upregulating GnRH receptor expression and sensitizing the pituitary to GnRH. This triggers a massive LH surge (and smaller FSH surge). The LH surge initiates the resumption of oocyte meiosis, cumulus expansion, ovarian wall breakdown, and ovulation ~36–40 hours later. The precise timing of this switch from negative to positive feedback makes the LH surge self-limiting — after ovulation, the corpus luteum produces both estrogen and progesterone, and progesterone restores negative feedback.
explanations.C: Progesterone is produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation — the corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle after the egg is released. Before ovulation (pre-surge and immediately post-surge), significant progesterone secretion has not yet occurred. The LH surge precedes and causes ovulation; progesterone rise follows ovulation.
explanations.D: If high estrogen decreased estrogen synthesis, that would be classic negative feedback — not the positive feedback switch described. The estrogen positive feedback (unusually stimulating more LH/FSH rather than suppressing them) is an exception to the usual endocrine negative feedback rule and is unique to this critical window in the menstrual cycle.

questions.topic: HPG Axis / LH Surge / Positive Feedback
